did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning methods, specifically convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and the MobileNetV2 architecture with transfer learning, to perform multimodal classification of skin neoplasms using the HAM10000 dataset. It also employed Grad-CAM for interpretability.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results, including the implementation and testing of specific deep learning models (Baseline CNN, Improved CNN, MobileNetV2, Multimodal MobileNetV2) on the HAM10000 dataset. It reports new quantitative metrics (e.g., test accuracy of 0.81 for the multimodal model) and includes sections for Introduction, Methods, Results, and Discussion, indicating it is an original research study rather than a review.

title: MULTIMODAL CLASSIFICATION OF SKIN NEOPLASMS BY USING DEEP LEARNING
journal: Journal of Information and Communication Systems (JICS)
brief_summary: This study evaluated deep learning methods for the multimodal classification of seven types of skin neoplasms using the HAM10000 dataset, which includes dermoscopic images and clinical metadata. The researchers implemented and compared a baseline CNN, an improved CNN with class balancing, a standard MobileNetV2 model, and a multimodal MobileNetV2 model integrating clinical metadata. Evaluation metrics included accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, with Grad-CAM used for interpretability to visualize model attention areas. The multimodal MobileNetV2 model achieved the highest test accuracy of 81%, significantly outperforming the baseline CNN's 39% and the improved CNN's 73%. Notably, the recall for melanoma increased from 0.38 in the baseline CNN to 0.60 in the multimodal model, reducing the risk of missing this dangerous disease. The results confirm the potential of deep learning in dermatological decision support while highlighting the need for further clinical validation.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification; 327 images (3.3%).

all_task_types.target: Seven discrete diagnostic categories of skin neoplasms (actinic keratosis/intradermal carcinoma, basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis-like lesions, dermatofibroma, melanoma, nevus, vascular lesions).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly performs multimodal classification of skin neoplasms using the HAM10000 dataset. The task is defined as multi-class classification with seven classes. Models output class predictions via softmax activation, and performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. The primary model (Multimodal MobileNetV2) achieved a test accuracy of 0.81.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A publicly available, open dataset widely recognized in the scientific community, serving as the experimental basis for the study. It contains 10,015 dermatoscopic images of seven types of skin neoplasms along with clinical metadata (gender, age, and lesion location) for some images.

use.training: Used for training baseline CNN, improved CNN, MobileNetV2, and multimodal MobileNetV2 models. Split into training and test subsets in an 80/20 ratio, with 10-20% of the training subset reserved as a validation set.
use.validation: A subset (10-20% of the training split) was used for validation via the validation_split option in Keras to monitor loss and apply early stopping.
use.testing: The remaining 20% of the dataset was used as the test set to evaluate model performance (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To mitigate strong class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset during model training.
To evaluate model performance and identify systematic errors between visually similar categories.
To report per-class precision, recall, and F1-score across all seven lesion categories.
To quantitatively evaluate and compare the performance of the baseline CNN, improved CNN, MobileNetV2, and multimodal MobileNetV2 models.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance comparisons were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, recall, precision, F1-score) without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.split: Test set (20% of total dataset, stratified by class)

Baseline CNN.notes: Trained from scratch on a small balanced subset of the HAM10000 dataset. No metadata, no class weighting.

Improved CNN.notes: Trained on the full HAM10000 dataset with class weighting and data augmentation. Systematic errors remained for rare categories (dermatofibroma, actinic keratosis) and confusion between melanoma and other pigmented lesions.

MobileNetV2.notes: Used only dermoscopic images with transfer learning and class weighting. Outperformed the baseline CNN trained on a small subset.

Multimodal MobileNetV2.notes: Combined image features with clinical metadata (sex, age, lesion location). Achieved the best overall performance. Melanoma recall increased from 0.38 (in Baseline CNN) to 0.60. Most errors arose between visually similar pigmented lesions (nevus, benign keratosis-like lesions, melanoma) and between benign keratosis-like lesions and actinic keratoses.

HAM10000.notes: The Multimodal MobileNetV2 model demonstrated a significant increase in completeness (recall) for the melanoma class compared to the baseline CNN, reducing the likelihood of missing the most dangerous type of tumors. Grad-CAM was used to visualize model attention areas.

architecture_used.main_model: MobileNetV2 (with transfer learning) and Baseline CNN (trained from scratch)
architecture_used.pretraining: MobileNetV2 backbone pre-trained on ImageNet; used as a fixed feature extractor for MobileNetV2-based models. Baseline CNN trained from scratch.
architecture_used.input_size: 128x128 pixels (images scaled and normalized to [0,1]); clinical metadata (gender, age, lesion location) as additional input.

baseline_cnn.structure: Convolutional blocks with 3x3 kernels (32 and 64 filters), BatchNormalization, MaxPooling2D, Flatten, two fully connected layers with 64 units and ReLU activation.
baseline_cnn.multimodal_variant: Image branch (64-dim feature vector) concatenated with metadata branch (64-dim embedding from dense layer with 64 units and ReLU). Concatenated 128-dim vector passed through dense layer (64 units, ReLU) and final classifier.

mobilenetv2_variant.structure: MobileNetV2 backbone (fixed feature extractor) followed by GlobalAveragePooling2D and the same multimodal fusion block as the improved CNN (metadata embedding concatenated with image features).

architecture_details.metadata_branch: Categorical variables one-hot encoded, normalized age, passed through dense layer (64 units, ReLU) to obtain 64-dim embedding.

training_details.class_imbalance_handling: Class weights inversely proportional to class frequencies.

classification_or_output_head.type: Dense layer with 7 units and softmax activation

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation: horizontal and vertical reflections (flips)
Class weighting (inversely proportional to class frequencies) to mitigate class imbalance
Stratified splitting of the dataset by class
One-hot encoding of categorical clinical metadata variables

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used as a standard augmentation technique during preprocessing to reduce overfitting. Applied to images scaled to 128x128 pixels.
Used as a standard augmentation technique during preprocessing to reduce overfitting.
Used as a standard augmentation technique during preprocessing to reduce overfitting.

Applied during training to combat class imbalance. Weights were inversely proportional to class frequencies to compensate for the predominance of nevus images and increase completeness for rare categories.

conclusions: The baseline CNN trained from scratch on a small balanced subset of the HAM10000 dataset showed limited performance, achieving a test accuracy of approximately 39%.

The improved CNN model, trained on the full HAM10000 dataset with class balancing and data augmentation, achieved a higher test accuracy of 73% but still exhibited limitations in classifying rare and visually similar categories.
The MobileNetV2 model, utilizing transfer learning and integrating clinical metadata (sex, age, and lesion location), demonstrated the best overall performance with a test accuracy of 81%.

The integration of clinical metadata significantly improved the recall for melanoma, increasing it from 0.38 in the baseline CNN to 0.60 in the multimodal model, thereby reducing the likelihood of missing this dangerous disease.

Grad-CAM was used to interpret model decisions, allowing for the visualization of attention areas and the identification of error causes, such as confusion between visually similar pigmented lesions (e.g., nevus, benign keratosis-like lesions, and melanoma).
The results confirm the potential of deep learning for decision support in dermatology but highlight the necessity for further clinical validation and expansion of the database.

Challenges in practical implementation include limited and inconsistent labeling in open datasets, pronounced class imbalance (predominance of nevus images), and differences in imaging devices and protocols that reduce model transferability to real-world clinical practice.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which has known limitations including significant class imbalance (67% nevus), duplicate images, and partial histological verification of diagnoses, potentially introducing bias.
The model was not externally validated on independent clinical sites or prospective data; the authors explicitly note the need for further clinical validation and expansion of the database.

There is a notable discrepancy in the MobileNetV2 performance: the abstract and text claim it integrates clinical metadata and achieves 81% accuracy, but Table 2 and the results section clarify that the standard MobileNetV2 used only images (53% accuracy) and the 81% accuracy belongs to the Multimodal MobileNetV2.

The baseline CNN was trained on a small balanced subset of the data, making its low accuracy (39%) an unfair comparison to models trained on the full dataset; however, it serves as a proof of concept for training from scratch.

The paper acknowledges that interpretability methods like Grad-CAM require critical review by experts to eliminate reliance on irrelevant features (e.g., artifacts) and does not provide quantitative validation of the explanations' clinical plausibility.

Real-world deployment is limited by differences in imaging devices, acquisition protocols, and patient populations, which reduce the transferability of models trained on a single dataset like HAM10000.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was performed; performance comparisons are based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, recall, F1-score) without formal hypothesis testing.
